It appears that IP litigation is an active segment of the market right now: in the past two months, Sheppard added 15 IP litigators from Perkins Coie, and Alston & Bird welcomed a quartet of IP litigators from Winston (both prior Moves of the Week).

As for other notable moves, two lawyers with valuable government experience found new jobs: Monica Freas left the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency and joined Skadden Arps in D.C., and former federal prosecutor Suneeta Hazra moved to the Denver office of WilmerHale. (Hazra joined Wilmer from Arnold & Porter Kaye Scholer, where she practiced for the past six-plus years; but before that, she spent almost two decades as an assistant U.S. attorney.)

Thanks for joining me for another edition of Judicial Notice. And to reward everyone who read this far, I’d like to offer you another helpful reminder (along the lines of last week’s suggestion to look yourself up in your state’s unclaimed-property database): if you have a fancy credit card like an American Express Platinum Card or Chase Sapphire Reserve (referral links), make sure to utilize all the benefits that are expiring at the end of this month (because they’re issued on a quarterly or semi-annual basis).

Happy spending! Because other than a cage match at the White House, what could be a more fitting way to celebrate America’s 250th anniversary than buying a ton of stuff and putting it on a credit card?

This post is only for paid subscribers of Original Jurisdiction . As always, thanks for your support!

item.title: Judicial Notice (06.07.26): One Angry Judge
item.description: This week’s Judicial Notice is sponsored by With a presence in over a dozen cities across the United States and Asia, Lateral Link boasts an expert recruiting team of former practicing attorneys dedicated to sourcing top-tier legal talent for a diverse clientele, including major international law…
item.author: David Lat
item.content:encoded:
This week’s Judicial Notice is sponsored by

With a presence in over a dozen cities across the United States and Asia, Lateral Link boasts an expert recruiting team of former practicing attorneys dedicated to sourcing top-tier legal talent for a diverse clientele, including major international law firms and Fortune 500 companies. To learn more about Lateral Link, please visit our website.

Last week took me to opposite ends of the Acela corridor. On Tuesday, I headed down to D.C. to moderate what was—as I predicted—a lively, even spicy panel discussion, “A More Perfect Union: Free Speech, DEI, and America at 250.” Then on Friday, I trekked up to Cambridge for my 30th college reunion at Harvard. Both events were a ton of fun, although I’m a bit exhausted right now—partly from all the travel, and partly from poor sleep the past two nights. (This was my first time staying in the dorms for one of my reunions, and it will definitely be the last.)

Here at Original Jurisdiction, and especially in Judicial Notice, I’m all about “news you can use.” So here’s a tip: look yourself up in the unclaimed-property database for your current state or any state where you previously resided. I did this for New York and New Jersey, found unclaimed funds for me and to Zach, and received checks totaling $200 in a matter of days. I also found money for my mother and for Zach’s dad. (You’re welcome! And if you’re a free subscriber to OJ and this reminder generates some cash for you, please consider upgrading to a paid subscription.)

Now, on to the news.

Lawyer of the Week: John Bolton.

Former national security adviser John Bolton is a lawyer by training, a Yale Law alumnus who worked at Covington & Burling and Kirkland & Ellis. But he’ll soon plead guilty to violating the law: as first reported by CNN, the Trump appointee turned adversary has reached a tentative plea agreement with federal prosecutors.

Under the deal, Bolton will avoid going to trial on an 18-count indictment—and the theoretical possibility of decades in prison if convicted at trial. Instead, he’ll plead guilty to a single count of illegal retention of classified information—specifically, materials he gathered to write his bestselling White House memoir, The Room Where It Happened—and pay a fine of $2.25 million, according to

After Bolton left the first Trump administration, following his ill-fated tenure as national security adviser, he became an outspoken critic of his ex-boss. So when Donald Trump returned to the presidency and started prosecuting his enemies, he unsurprisingly targeted Bolton—just as he went after James Comey and Letitia James.

But unlike the prosecutions of Jim Comey and Tish James, “Bolton’s case has maintained the support of career prosecutors and investigators,” per CNN. Why? As noted by Sarah Isgur and David French of Advisory Opinions, the case against Bolton appears to have at least some basis in law and fact (for reasons explained by Andrew McCarthy of National Review). In light of this, Bolton and his lawyer, Abbe Lowell, presumably weighed the odds and concluded that cutting a deal was the wisest course of action. (I also wonder if they’re counting on Bolton’s age—he’s 77—as a factor favoring leniency at sentencing.)

Other lawyers in the news:

  • Unlike Bolton, former Supreme Court advocate

    Tom Goldsteindeclined a deal—which, according to Goldstein, called for a prison sentence ofroughly five years. Will he regret that choice? It could depend on what happens at sentencing, currently scheduled before JudgeLydia Griggsby(D. Md.) on June 16. In duelingsentencing submissions, the government advocated for 97 months in prison, while the defense argued for no prison time.

  • In a follow-up on last week’s featured lawyers, the 35 ex-judges who filed a brief attacking Trump’s Anti-Weaponization Fund, Adam Liptak laid out the arguments for and against such filings by former judges in his newsletter,

    The Docket. See also Elie Honig’sNew York Magazinecolumn—in which OJ gets a shout-out.

  • Speaking of the Fund, it’s not happening—as Acting Attorney General

    Todd Blanchetestified to Congress, and the Department of Justice subsequently confirmed in a pair of court filings (viaThe Washington Post(gift link)).

  • Maybe the Fund would have died anyway, given how politically unpopular it was. But legal challenges might have hastened its demise; Donald Trump

    complainedto conservative podcaster Miranda Devine that although he still supported the Fund, “a court ruled against it.”

  • If past editions of JN are any guide, readers love clicking on articles about

    George T. Conway, theWachtell Liptonpartner turned Trump archenemy turned Democratic congressional candidate. So here’s another—a profile of GTC by Emma Goldberg ofThe New York Times(gift link).

  • Speaking of The Times, it published detailed pieces on

    judges benchslapping Trump administration lawyersand the not-unrelated development oflawyers leaving the administration—in large numbers.

  • You know who’s

    notleaving the Trump Justice Department? Acting AG Todd Blanche—whom Trump said he plans to nominate to serve as the Senate-confirmed AG, as reported in numerous news articles (collected by Howard Bashman atHow Appealing).

  • If you spent even five minutes on LinkedIn last week, you’re aware that the

    Chambers USA 2026 guideis out. So congratulations to all the lawyers and law firms recognized in the latest Chambers rankings, upon which I place great weight (e.g., when deciding whether a partner move is worth mentioning in JN).

  • And kudos to the 200 attorneys featured on Forbes’s list of

    America’s Top Women Lawyers—especially the 10 who have appeared as guests on my podcast.

  • Speaking of high-powered women attorneys,

    Kathryn “Kathy” Ruemmleris supposed to step down as the top lawyer at Goldman Sachs at the end of this month, in the wake of controversy over her ties to Jeffrey Epstein. But at the request of CEO David Solomon, it appears she’llremain involvedin the bank’s legal affairs in an advisory role.

Judge of the Week: Judge Ryan Nelson.

One of my most notorious posts at my very first blog, Underneath Their Robes, was titled State Court Judges Are Icky. After reviewing a slew of stories about state judges embroiled in scandals, I argued that compared to their federal counterparts, state judges “seem to have a knack for getting themselves into hot water.”

Alas, based on 2026 to date, federal judges seem pretty icky too. "Underneath Their Robes could be resurrected—not as a blog, but as a bad reality TV show.

In April, Judge Thomas Ludington (E.D. Mich.) pleaded no contest to a misdemeanor drunken-driving charge—and has been ordered back to court tomorrow, for a possible probation violation (an alleged failure to submit to drug testing). In May, the world learned about the misadventures of Judge Eleanor Ross (N.D. Ga.), who engaged in an extramarital affair with a high-ranking law enforcement officer—and had noisy sex with him in chambers, within hearing distance of her staff.

This brings us to today—and Judge Ryan Nelson, who has served on the Ninth Circuit since his 2018 appointment by Donald Trump. On Friday, the Idaho State Journal reported that Judge Nelson has been charged with battery and malicious injury to property. The charges, both misdemeanors, arise out of an alleged incident that took place in an Idaho Falls parking lot on April 2 (and was captured on video).

According to the Journal (via Bloomberg Law), Nelson got into an argument with another Idaho Falls resident, after the man made a disparaging remark about the judge’s parking job. The verbal dispute escalated into a physical altercation—during which Nelson allegedly swiped sunglasses from the man’s face (alleged battery), then tossed them across the parking lot and stomped on them (alleged injury to property).

This isn’t Judge Nelson’s first vehicular-related brush with the law. Back in 2018, shortly after his confirmation, The Wall Street Journal (gift link) reported on the new judge’s 28 traffic citations over the past two decades, including around a dozen speeding tickets. He was found or pleaded guilty in about two-thirds of the cases, while successfully challenging others. (The WSJ did add that “[o]utwardly, Mr. Nelson shows no signs of being either a speedster or a scofflaw,” pointing out that he’s a triathlete, a volunteer with the Boy Scouts, and an Eagle Scout himself.)

I reached out to Judge Nelson for comment on this unfortunate occurrence. His counsel, Curtis Smith, sent me the following statement: “Mr. Nelson is embarrassed by this incident. It is out of character and does not represent how he behaves. Immediately afterwards, Mr. Nelson reached out and offered an apology and full compensation for the sunglasses. He intends to work through the proper process.”

I actually met Judge Nelson years ago, at a conference. We sat together at lunch, he was delightful company, and I emerged from our time together with a positive impression (and my eyeglasses intact). Based on this brief but pleasant interaction, as well as positive things I’ve heard about him from mutual friends, I was surprised by both the charges and the video (which is—not gonna lie—not great).

And folks who know Judge Nelson well have come to his defense (a contrast to the relative silence regarding Judges Ludington and Ross). For example, over at the Volokh Conspiracy, Professor Josh Blackman wrote, “All of my interactions with [Nelson] over the years have been very cordial. The person I saw in that video was not the person I have come to know and like.”

I also heard from two former clerks to Judge Nelson, readers of Original Jurisdiction who reached out to me (expecting—correctly—that I’d be covering this). I was glad to hear from them because one of the first things I wondered, based on the angry man I saw in the video, was whether he flies off the handle in chambers. From the first clerk:

  • “He was a terrific judge to clerk for—kind and helpful, and a great mentor. The video does not reflect the judge that I know.”

  • “The sharpest criticism I ever received from him was when he got on my case after I was too harsh on a co-clerk. He always stressed the importance of a professional and collegial environment in chambers.”

  • “I love him to death. He’s a good man—and I’m heartbroken for him that this one incident will now show up prominently whenever people Google him.”

And from the second clerk:

  • “He was incredibly kind to me during my clerkship. He cares very deeply about the people who work with him. And he’s not an angry person—so I was shocked when I saw the video.”

  • “What I saw was incredibly out of character for the man I know and have come to admire and respect. The only thing I can chalk it up to is a bad day.”

On the subject of a bad day, the first clerk shared with me that Judge Nelson has been going through a difficult time personally right now: “His father recently had a heart attack (which he thankfully survived), and other family members have been dealing with cancer. I haven’t spoken to him about the incident, so I don’t know if any of this played into it, but I can certainly imagine it.”

Josh Blackman did some sleuthing, and based on the surveillance footage and Google Maps, he concluded that the parking lot where the incident transpired belongs to a Home and Hospice Center. So it certainly seems at least possible that Judge Nelson wasn’t in a good state of mind when he had the parking argument.

Regardless of what caused Judge Nelson to behave in this way, this is definitely not the kind of conduct we want to see from judges (or, for that matter, anyone else). But as someone who sucks at parking, made a trip to traffic court last year, and doesn’t always have the best temper, I’m not in the best position to judge Judge Nelson. So I’ll close by quoting Blackman: “As a general rule, we should not be judged at our worst moment—though in life and law, sometimes we are.”

In nominations news:

  • By identical 52-46 votes, the Senate

    confirmedtwo district-court nominees (and Scalia Law alums): former Montana deputy solicitor generalKathleen “Katie” Lane(D. Mont.) and civil litigatorJeffrey Kuhlman(D. Kan.).

  • Senator John Fetterman of Pennsylvania became the first Democrat to turn in a

    blue slipfor a second-term Trump nominee, allowingFaegre DrinkerpartnerAntonio Pozos(E.D. Pa.) to get a hearing before the Senate Judiciary Committee.

Job of the Week: an opportunity for litigation associates in Houston or Austin.

Lateral Link is partnering with a premier litigation boutique expanding its Texas platform—seeking litigation associates with 2020-2025 JDs for offices in Houston and Austin. The firm handles sophisticated, high-stakes matters and offers early hands-on experience, including depositions, witness examinations, and trial work rarely available at larger firms. Ideal candidates have exceptional credentials from a T14 or similarly prestigious law school, strong grades, and a judicial clerkship, though top-tier Biglaw litigation experience will also be considered. The firm offers New York market compensation, clerkship bonuses, robust mentorship, personalized onboarding, and a clear path to partnership within a collaborative, diverse leadership culture. Contact Wendy Boone at wendyboone@laterallink.com to learn more.

Ruling of the Week: Talbott v. United States .

The Supreme Court’s most significant action last week took place on the emergency docket. In Alabama v. Milligan, the (seemingly interminable) litigation over what congressional map Alabama can use in its upcoming elections, a majority of justices gave the green light to the state using its preferred (Republican-friendly) map. Justice

As for argued cases, the Court issued three merits opinions last Thursday. But as you know if you joined us on the SCOTUSblog live blog, they were less than scintillating, so I’ll skip discussing them here and simply refer you to SCOTUSblog.

While we wait for bigger decisions from the high court, and in a nod to June being Pride Month, let’s talk about Talbott v. United States. As you might recall from my

Last Monday, a divided panel of the D.C. Circuit ruled for the plaintiffs—in part. Judges Robert Wilkins and Judith Rogers voted to affirm the portion of the preliminary injunction issued by Judge Ana Reyes (D.D.C.) that blocked the Hegseth Policy as to individuals already serving—and also to modify the PI to apply only to the 28 named plaintiffs in

What happens next? As explained by the Associated Press, the D.C. Circuit stayed its ruling to allow the administration to seek Supreme Court review—which it probably will, as suggested by Hegseth’s “See you at SCOTUS” tweet. As to what will happen at One First Street, my guess is that a majority of the Court will vote to uphold the Hegseth Policy—based largely on how the justices handled an emergency-docket appeal in a separate case about the Hegseth Policy last May, in which they let the ban take effect. (As Minter explained, to both The Times and to me last year, there are important doctrinal differences between Talbott and the other case, Shilling v. Trump—but in my experience, when the Court issues an emergency-docket ruling favoring a particular policy outcome, it sticks to that outcome, legal nuances notwithstanding.)

The issues in Talbott are complicated—reflected in how the panel produced three separate opinions, totaling 107 pages. So for now, I’ll close with the words of Shannon Minter: “This will have an immediate positive impact on the lives of people who just want to serve their country, but have been through a long, dispiriting ordeal. My clients feel vindicated that a court said what happened to them is wrong.”

Other noteworthy decisions and dispositions:

  • Last week’s benchslaps of attorneys for abusing AI assistance are brought to you by the

    Seventh Circuit(opinion by Chief JudgeMichael Brennan) and theNinth Circuit(unsigned order).

  • From John Ross of

    Short Circuit, here’s a summary of the Ninth Circuit’s en banc opinion in a case with a great caption,: “Gov’t: We took a million dollars from this guy at a traffic stop and we’re going to forfeit it unless he provides an extremely detailed written explanation of exactly where it came from and how he earned it. This guy: I don’t want to write you, like, a novel. I just want to argue that the traffic stop violated the Fourth Amendment and that you have to give my money back. Ninth Circuit [by JudgeUnited States v. $1,106,775.00 In United States CurrencyDaniel Bress]: And he gets to do that.”

  • Judge

    Randolph “Randy” Moss(D.D.C.) issued atemporary restraining orderprohibiting the Trump administration from forcing protesters to stop flying a flag reading “8647” at their 24/7 anti-Trump vigil on Constitution Avenue in D.C.

Litigation of the Week: National Trust for Historic Preservation v. National Park Service .

The D.C. Circuit is also the source of the Litigation of the Week: National Trust for Historic Preservation v. National Park Service, aka the White House ballroom lawsuit, in which oral argument took place last Friday. For collected coverage and a link to the argument recording, check out How Appealing.

The two Democratic appointees on the panel, Judges Patricia Millett and Brad Garcia, seemed sympathetic to the arguments challenging Trump’s demolition of the historic East Wing to make way for a $400 million ballroom project. But the Republican appointee, Judge Neomi Rao, zeroed in on the issue of standing, questioning whether the National Trust for Historic Preservation and one of its members, Alison Hoagland, had suffered the kind of concrete, particularized injury allowing them to sue in federal court.

Principal Deputy Assistant Attorney General Yaakov Roth argued that the construction couldn’t be halted at this point—and received tough questions from Judge Millett. She asked, “So if this were complete lawlessness by ​the government, it couldn't be stopped?” Roth responded, “I think that’s right.”

At a different point in the argument, Judge Millett offered a quip that strikes me as one of the best summaries of the Trump administration’s position—not just in this case, but many others. She characterized the government’s approach as follows: “So just move fast and break things, then nobody has standing.”

I don’t believe she meant this as a compliment. But as a litigation strategy, the administration’s reliance on defenses such as standing, ripeness, mootness, and jurisdiction has proved successful in a non-trivial number of cases. And if National Trust for Historic Preservation makes its way to SCOTUS via the emergency docket, I’d bet on the administration prevailing—for reasons separate from the merits.

In other litigation news:

  • As required by an order issued by Judge

    Richard Eatonof the Court of International Trade, the government has started processing refund requests and paying out money. But the administration ispushing backon the across-the-board nature of Judge Eaton’s order, taking the position that some importers need to file individual lawsuits to get refunds. An angry Judge Eaton issued an order requiring the head of U.S. Customs and Border Protection to testify in his court this coming Tuesday to clarify the government’s position—but the Federal Circuitput that on hold, at least for now.

  • Ohio State University reached a

    preliminary agreementto pay $100 million to 279 former students who allege they were sexually abused by a former team doctor.

Deal of the Week: Berkshire Hathaway’s $6.8 billion acquisition of Taylor Morrison.

What does the future hold for Berkshire Hathaway without Warren Buffett at the helm? Under new CEO Greg Abel, Berkshire announced one of its biggest moves in the post-Buffett era: using $6.8 billion of its $381 billion war chest to acquire Taylor Morrison, a homebuilder and developer. The law firms are Gibson Dunn for Berkshire, Simpson Thacher and Mayer Brown for Taylor Morrison, and Sullivan & Cromwell for Goldman Sachs and Moelis, Taylor Morrison’s financial advisers.

In other deal news, Barry Diller’s People Inc. offered to buy the 73.9% of MGM Resorts that it doesn’t already own, in a bid valuing MGM at $18 billion (including debt). This comes on the heels of last week’s news that Fertitta Entertainment will acquire Caesars for $17.6 billion (also including debt), reflecting consolidation in the gaming industry.

Law Firm of the Week: Milbank.

Oops, they did it again: on Tuesday, Milbank—which my former colleagues at Above the Law have nicknamed “Thrillbank”—raised associate salaries. The new scale starts at $235,000 for first-year associates, up from $225,000, and climbs to $455,000 for eight-year associates, up from $435,000. It takes effect on July 1 (and yes, summer associates will enjoy the benefits of the new scale).

In its write-up of the news, Bloomberg Law referred to Milbank as “Biglaw’s first mover when it comes to associate salaries, ”and that’s borne out by recent years. As noted by ATL’s Kathryn Rubino, Milbank led the charge in June 2018, June 2021, January 2022, and November 2023.

Why did Milbank do it? It’s probably a combination of “because they can”—with revenue per lawyer north of $2 million, a pay bump of $10K to $20K is pocket change—and because they see an opportunity to move up in the prestige rankings. In the latest Vault ranking, Milbank was #9 “with a bullet,” rising four spots when no other firm in the top 10 moved by more than one. So it might see an opportunity to build on that momentum and overtake some higher-ranked competitors that have suffered from reputational hits in the past year (cough cough, Paul Weiss).

Which firms have matched? So far, in terms of Biglaw firms, we’ve heard from McDermott, Quinn Emanuel, and Katten, per ATL’s handy associate compensation scorecard (which also includes midyear bonuses announced by the litigation boutiques Dunn Isaacson Rhee, Hueston Hennigan, and Selendy Gay).

Might we see a rival go over the top and beat the new Milbank scale? Looking at the usual players, I doubt it. Cravath’s financial performance in 2025 was weaker than Milbank’s, in terms of revenue per lawyer and profits per equity partner. That reality, combined with all of Cravath’s partner departures in 2026 to date, might make the firm reluctant to splurge on associate pay.

Davis Polk did do better than Milbank last year, posting percentage increases in RPL and PPEP that were more than twice Milbank’s. But DPW has been investing in high-profile partner hires like Kannon Shanmugam and Jon Oram, so it might want to take a breather. And considering how hot Davis is as a firm right now, it doesn’t need to beat Milbank to remain a destination of choice for associates; it can merely match, and recruits will still flock to 450 Lex.

In other firm news:

  • Speaking of Davis Polk, Sujeet Indap of the Financial Times

    tweetedabout the firm’s billing rates, including hourly rates of $1,175 and $1,410 for first- and second-year associates, respectively. The tweet went viral, racking up more than a million views. Some readers flagged it for me with shock or indignation, but I shrugged: Biglaw gonna Biglaw.

  • Top-tier Biglaw M&A practices have been killing it—and now capital-markets practices are getting in on the action, through a wave of

    high-profile IPOs(including that ofSpaceX, frontrunner for next Deal of the Week, featuring Gibson Dunn for SpaceX and Davis Polk for the underwriters).

  • Is

    Weil Gotshalmissing the boat on the latest Biglaw boom? Roy Strom did a deep dive into Weil forBloomberg Law, which will undergo a crucial transition whenRamona NeesucceedsBarry Wolfas executive partner at the end of 2026.

  • It’s official: please refer to the firms formerly known as Winston & Strawn and Taylor Wessing as

    Winston Taylor, which officiallylaunchedlast Monday—as a transatlantic law firm with more than 1,400 lawyers and $1.75 billion in revenue.

  • A

    lawsuitfiled in New York Supreme Court accusesPillsbury Winthropand one of its California-based partners,Riaz Karamali, of allegedly assisting a $145 million fraud. In a statement, Pillsbury said that the claims “are frivolous and lack any merit,” and “we will vigorously defend ourselves in court.”
  • Morgan & Morgan—the largest personal-injury firm in the United States, with more than $2 billion in annual revenue—isexploringthe possible sale of a minority stake to investors. Any such sale would have to be structured in a way that complies with ethical and regulatory requirements, perhaps by taking the form of the sale of back-office functions (unless the regulations are changed).

Move of the Week: White & Case hiring five lateral partners, including Anirudh Bansal from Cahill and Dario de Martino from A&O Shearman.

Back in January, I wrote about the strategic plan of White & Case, which targets hitting $5 billion in revenue in three years. A key part of the plan, of course, is lateral partner hiring. So I wasn’t surprised to see lateral news out of White & Case last week, which reflects an attempt to execute on the plan.

Last Monday, White & Case announced the hiring of five lateral partners, across three offices and four practice groups, as reported by Bloomberg Law and Law.com:

  • The New York office welcomed two Chambers-ranked partners who held leadership roles in their former firms:

    Anirudh Bansal, chair ofCahill Gordon’s white-collar group, andDario De Martino, co-chair ofA&O Shearman’s global fintech and blockchain group.Foley & Lardnertax partnerDavid Maksoalso came aboard in the Big Apple.

  • White & Case hired a second fintech partner,

    David G. Adams, who moved over fromMintz Levinin Washington, D.C.

  • And in Chicago,

    Jalpit Aminjoined the M&A practice and technology industry team, hopping fromHogan Lovells.

Other notable moves:

  • Willkie Farrpicked upa pair of private-equity partners fromGoodwinin California:Stephen Lee, in Los Angeles, andBrian Raynor, in San Francisco.
  • Cravathmade its first partner hire of 2026,hiringMichael Patrick Dalyin D.C. (but note that he came from the National Security Division of the Justice Department, as opposed to a rival firm).

  • After arguing some of the Trump administration’s biggest circuit-court appeals—including

    Talbott, discussed above, as well as the Biglaw executive-orders cases—former deputy associate attorney generalAbhishek “Abhi” KamblijoinedHoltzman Vogel, working out of the D.C. and New York offices.

As always, thanks for your readership and support. I wish everyone a great week ahead. And for fellow residents of the northeast, get your AC—home and car—ready for the heat that’s heading our way.

This post is only for paid subscribers of Original Jurisdiction . As always, thanks for your support!

item.title: Judicial Notice (06.07.26): One Angry Judge
item.description: This week’s Judicial Notice is sponsored by With a presence in over a dozen cities across the United States and Asia, Lateral Link boasts an expert recruiting team of former practicing attorneys dedicated to sourcing top-tier legal talent for a diverse clientele, including major international law…

This week’s Judicial Notice is sponsored by

With a presence in over a dozen cities across the United States and Asia, Lateral Link boasts an expert recruiting team of former practicing attorneys dedicated to sourcing top-tier legal talent for a diverse clientele, including major international law firms and Fortune 500 companies. To learn more about Lateral Link, please visit our website.

On Tuesday, I headed down to D.C. to moderate what was—as I predicted—a lively, even spicy panel discussion, “A More Perfect Union: Free Speech, DEI, and America at 250.” Then on Friday, I trekked up to Cambridge for my 30th college reunion at Harvard. (This was my first time staying in the dorms for one of my reunions, and it will definitely be the last.)
