But the talks failed after creditors objected to how they’d be treated under the deal, per the WSJ (gift link). So on Saturday, Spirit Airlines shut down permanently, canceling all its flights. Spirit Airlines, RIP.

Law Firm of the Week: King & Spalding.

Besides litigation shops like Quinn Emanuel, Williams & Connolly, Susman Godfrey, Kellogg Hansen, and Boies Schiller, which Am Law 200 firms remain committed to lit? I’d mention—among others, this list of three is far from exhaustive—Gibson Dunn, Jones Day, and my latest Law Firm of the Week, King & Spalding.

For his latest Litigators of the Week, Ross Todd of Am Law Litigation Daily selected K&S partners Lazar Raynal, Craig Carpenito, and Mike Lombardo. They represented Skillz, the publicly traded maker of a skill-based gaming app, which accused a competitor, Papaya, of false advertising. Specifically, Skillz alleged that Papaya utilized bots as “players” in games that it falsely advertised as skill-based, in violation of the Lanham Act. After a two-week trial before Judge Denise Cote (S.D.N.Y.), a jury agreed with Skillz—and awarded $420 million in damages.

Skillz Platform Inc. v. Papaya Gaming Ltd. is a good example of a trend I recently wrote about: corporations, including public companies, becoming more comfortable with being plaintiffs. And King & Spalding is a good example of an elite law firm moving beyond traditional defense work to handle affirmative litigation, often on contingency (a subject I’ll be moderating a panel about on Wednesday morning, for Am Law 200 litigation partners who are interested in learning more).

As reflected in the latest Am Law 100 rankings, King & Spalding is #18 in revenue per lawyer and #12 in profits per equity partner. When you’re this high in the rankings, the competition is fierce. If K&S took Skillz v. Papaya on contingency—whether partial or full—it could be a nice boost for its 2026 financials.

Move of the Week: Sheppard adding 15 IP litigators from Perkins Coie.

Intellectual property litigators are flocking to Sheppard. After pulling a 21-lawyer IP litigation group from Ropes & Gray last September and adding three partners from Perkins Coie last June, Sheppard returned to Perkins and picked up another 15 IP litigators. Led by five partners—Veronica Ascarrunz, Robin Brewer, Andrew Klein, Tom Millikan, and Joseph Reid—the group includes attorneys in San Francisco, Silicon Valley, Southern California, and Washington, D.C. For more on the move, see Bloomberg Law, Law.com, and Law360.

Other notable moves:

  • Vinson & Elkinswelcomedfour corporate partners fromWilmerHale:Keith Trammell,Chalyse Robinson, andDavid Strong, in Denver, andAlex Bahn, in D.C.

  • Continuing to poach partners from other top firms,

    Paul HastingshiredAmanda PersaudfromRopes & Grayin New York. She will co-chair the rapidly growing funds and private-capital practice at Paul Hastings.

  • How to cut deals like a porn star: serving as the

    general counsel to OnlyFanshas to be one of the more interesting in-house gigs out there. And it will soon be held byDavid Eisman, who’s leavingSkadden Arpsto assume the position.
  • Jason Manion, former counselor to the U.S. attorney general, is departing from the DOJ andjoiningthe new White House Task Force to Eliminate Fraud, where he’ll serve as its general counsel.

Thanks for joining me for this latest edition of Judicial Notice. And for those of you who will be in New York City this coming Wednesday, May 6, please join me for a panel discussion about Biglaw’s move into contingency litigation, hosted by Burford Capital. Breakfast will be provided—as will CLE credit, courtesy of Lawline—and I look forward to seeing some of you there.

This post is only for paid subscribers of Original Jurisdiction . As always, thanks for your support!

item.title: The Top 20 Most Profitable Law Firms (2025)
item.description: This post is sponsored by Burford Capital helps companies and law firms unlock the value of their legal assets. With a portfolio of over $7 billion and listings on the NYSE and LSE, Burford provides capital to finance high-value commercial litigation and arbitration—without adding cost or risk or…
item.author: David Lat
item.content:encoded:
This post is sponsored by

Burford Capital helps companies and law firms unlock the value of their legal assets. With a portfolio of over $7 billion and listings on the NYSE and LSE, Burford provides capital to finance high-value commercial litigation and arbitration—without adding cost or risk or giving up control. Clients include Fortune 500 companies and Am Law 100 firms, who turn to Burford to pursue strong claims, manage legal costs and accelerate recoveries. Learn more at burfordcapital.com.

When I recently mentioned the compensation paid to the most coveted lateral partners, some readers—especially the older ones, who recall when $6 million was at or near the top of the market, circa 20 years ago—were incredulous.

Is it really true that Jeff Wall, SCOTUS advocate extraordinaire, is getting $15 million a year from Gibson Dunn, as reported by The Wall Street Journal (gift link)? Or that some Biglaw partners earn as much as $40 million a year, according to Law360 and Law.com?

Yes, Virginia, there is a Santa Claus. And he comes down the chimneys of partners with giant bags full of cash.

And Santa Claus, whom I’m playfully invoking because of the famous quotation, isn’t the right comparison. Santa brings gifts; partner compensation is earned.

As David Boies once told me—speaking about associate bonuses, but the same point applies to partner pay—“law firms are not eleemosynary institutions.” They pay high compensation, to associates and partners alike, because it’s in their self-interest.

Some senior readers who were skeptical of huge pay packages invoked Dewey & LeBoeuf—which doled out crazy compensation, to both lateral and homegrown partners, before collapsing and filing for bankruptcy in 2012. They asked: Dewey now have a bubble in partner pay on our hands?

There’s no doubt that some individual partners are overpaid. If an expensive lateral hire leaves a firm after only two or three years, sometimes it’s because they had guaranteed comp for a certain period, the guarantee ran out, and their pay was set to drop (because they’re not generating enough revenue at their new firm to justify their desired pay under the firm’s standard comp system).

But I wouldn’t say there’s a partner-pay bubble overall. Unlike Dewey, many if not most high-paying firms have the financial wherewithal to be offering what they’re offering. And, in fact, these firms need to compensate partners at these levels, to secure the talent they need to stay competitive.

And what’s my basis for saying this? The 2026 Am Law 100 rankings, published earlier this month by The American Lawyer.

The rankings and their supporting data show that elite firms are doing quite well for themselves, thank you very much. So these firms are investing in their futures by onboarding more and more superstars, which creates a virtuous cycle: great lateral hires increase firm profitability, enabling the firms that excel at it to use their higher profitability and growing war chests to attract even more top talent.

Let’s start with the big picture for Biglaw, reflected in certain key metrics (based on firms’ financial performances in calendar year 2025):

Total gross revenue: $178.95 billion, up 13.0%

Revenue per lawyer (RPL): $1.39 million, up 8.7%

Profits per equity partner (PEP): $3.59 million, up 14.0%

No, these numbers aren’t adjusted for inflation. But with inflation at around 2.7% in 2025, Biglaw beat it by a multiple of three to five.

And how did 2025 stack up compared to recent years? Here’s some data:

In his analysis of the latest rankings, Patrick Smith of The American Lawyer accurately observed that “Am Law 100 firms, for the most part, reproduced their stellar year from 2024.” And that’s nothing to complain about, since 2024 was the best year for Biglaw since the banner year of 2021 (when the industry was bouncing back from the short, but deep, pandemic-induced recession).

One metric where 2024 outpaced 2025 was headcount. In 2024, the attorney population of the Am Law 100 grew by 7.7%; in 2025, headcount grew at around half that pace, by 4%. (In case you’re curious, it’s now at 128,868 attorneys—a reminder that even if Biglaw dominates the pages of Original Jurisdiction, its lawyers account for a small share of America’s 1.4 million lawyers.)

Not all Biglaw attorney populations grew at the same rate—and the differing rates help explain the growth in profits per equity partner. The ranks of nonequity partners increased by almost 7%, while the number of equity partners grew by only 2%. The overall headcount growth of 4% is a blend of the growth rates of equity partners, nonequity partners, and associates.

Because overall headcount grew by 4% but the number of equity partners grew by only 2%, leverage—defined as the ratio of (1) lawyers who aren’t equity partners to (2) equity partners—increased as well. And increased leverage generally means increased profits per equity partner, because the profit generated by each lawyer who’s not an equity partner winds up in the pockets of the equity partners.

Up until now, we’ve been discussing the collective performance of Biglaw, which conceals a fair amount of variation. Now let’s look at how individual firms fared, in terms of gross revenue, revenue per lawyer, and profits per equity partner—and maybe gossip a bit about what some of the numbers say about different firms.

And yes, I will explain how firms get to $30 million and $40 million partner paydays….

Gross Revenue

The Am Law 100 ranking is a ranking of the largest law firms in the United States based on gross revenue. So let’s eat our salad first and look at the revenue ranking—before we turn to the entree of revenue per lawyer, followed by the dessert of profits per equity partner.

Collectively, the Am Law 100 grew their total gross revenue by 13 percent, which provides one possible benchmark for evaluating the performance of individual firms (although it’s not a perfect apples-to-apples comparison, since the firms that make up the Am Law 100 change slightly from year to year). If a firm increased its gross revenue by more than 13 percent, it’s fair to say that firm had a strong year.

In 2025, 62 firms had $1 billion or more in revenue in 2025, compared to 58 in 2024. And 94 firms in the Am Law 100 firm grew their gross revenue last year—another data point confirming that 2025, on the whole, treated Biglaw well. Only two firms witnessed revenue drops (and four firms were new to the Am Law 100 this year, so they had no year-over-year change).

Here are the top 20 firms in the 2026 Am Law 100, ranked by their gross revenue in 2025. You can access the full list at The American Lawyer.

As usual, there was a lot of stability in the top 20:

As noted by Am Law, the top 10 was fairly stable, with seven firms not moving at all. Two firms, Gibson Dunn and Skadden Arps, swapped places (at #4 and #5). Simpson Thacher climbed two spots, to break into the top 10; Morgan Lewis dropped by one, to #11.

In the top 20 firms, 19 of the firms were returnees. The newcomer, at #16, was McDermott Will & Schulte, created through the merger of McDermott Will & Emery (formerly #23) and Schulte Roth & Zabel (formerly #91). The firm that left the top 20 to make room for MWS was Goodwin, which went from #19 to #21.

For the ninth year in a row, Kirkland & Ellis was the #1 firm in revenue. It grew its revenue by almost 20%, to more than $10.5 billion, becoming the first firm in history to break the $10 billion mark. And three firms in the top 20 boasted even larger percentage gains than Kirkland: Simpson Thacher (22.7%), Paul Weiss (23.8%), and Davis Polk (26.0%—the biggest jump in the top 20).

We don’t have the Fortune 500 ranking based on 2025 financial performance yet. But if we use the ranking based on 2024 financial performance, the cutoff for inclusion was $7.4 billion in revenue—meaning that Kirkland and Latham would be Fortune 500 members, if law firms were eligible for inclusion.

Breaking into the top 20 required almost $2.8 billion in revenue in 2025, compared to $2.4 billion in 2024.

Moving beyond the top 20, two firms in the Am Law 100 experienced revenue drops: Fragomen (-5.50%) and Cadwalader Wickersham & Taft (-3.35%). Fragomen is a high-end immigration firm that does a lot of work for corporate clients on employment-based visas, so it was hurt by the Trump administration’s policy changes—especially the $100,000 fee for H-1B visas. As for Cadwalader, its troubles have been chronicled extensively in these pages, but it saved itself through a merger with Hogan Lovells (so next year, CWT won’t be in the Am Law 100 as an independent firm).

There were four newcomers to the Am Law 100, all fueled by mergers: McDermott, discussed above; Troutman Pepper Locke, forged in January 2025 through the merger of Troutman Pepper (#52) and Locke Lord (#99); Taft Stettinius & Hollister, which has grown through a series of mergers and acquisitions (most recently, its rescue of Morris Manning & Martin); and Womble Bond Dickinson, which merged with Lewis Roca.

Per Am Law, the firm that grew revenue the most without a merger was Littler Mendelson, whose revenue skyrocketed by 40.89% to $1.029 billion. Like Fragomen, Littler was also affected by changes in law and policy: as a labor and employment powerhouse, Littler benefited from the first year of the Trump administration, which forced employers to deal with legal issues related to civil rights, discrimination, DEI, and immigration.

After Littler, seven other firms grew revenue by more than 20%: Shook Hardy & Bacon (28.43%), Ballard Spahr (27.63%), Davis Polk (25.98%), Polsinelli (25.52%), Paul Weiss (23.8%), Susman Godfrey (23.17%), and Simpson (22.7%).

What can be said about the four firms that fought executive orders from the Trump administration, including Susman, and the nine firms that reached settlements, including Paul Weiss and Simpson? As reported by Abigail Adcox of Am Law, seven out of the nine settling firms saw double-digit revenue growth (with only Cadwalader seeing a decline, and A&O Shearman not reporting yet).

Results were mixed for the four fighting firms: Susman (23.17%), Jenner & Block (14.86%), Perkins Coie (2.0%), and WilmerHale (0.6%). The EOs, enjoined by judges, never went into effect—so I’m guessing they didn’t have much of an impact on the firms’ business. Rather, their performances can be chalked up to other factors; for example, WilmerHale does a fair amount of white-collar defense and investigations work, which has been slow under Trump.

Revenue Per Lawyer

I agree with The American Lawyer: revenue per lawyer is “the most reliable measure of a firm’s financial health.” It’s not as easy to increase as gross revenue, which a firm can juice through mass hiring or mergers, or profits per equity partner, which a firm can hack by shrinking its equity partnership aka “de-equitizing.”

It was a good year for Biglaw in terms of RPL. For the Am Law 100 as a whole, RPL increased by 8.7%, and the gains were widespread, with 92 firms growing RPL.

Here are the top 20 firms in the 2026 Am Law 100 ranking, ranked by their revenue per lawyer in 2025. You can access the full list at The American Lawyer.

The top 10 firms in RPL stayed the same, simply changing positions among themselves. But the #1 and #2 firms stayed the same: Wachtell Lipton, with a mind-boggling $5 million in RPL, and Susman Godfrey, with $2.9 million in RPL.

In discussing the recent Vault ranking of the most prestigious firms, I wrote that Wachtell Lipton is arguably the nation’s #1 corporate firm, and Susman Godfrey is arguably the nation’s #1 litigation firm. I’d argue that it’s no coincidence that they’re #1 and #2 in RPL. See why I like RPL as a measure of a firm quality?

The top 20 firms in RPL were almost entirely the same, again just changing places within the top 20. There was only one newcomer: Dechert, which climbed from #21 to #16. The firm that dropped out of the top 20 to make room for Dechert was Sidley Austin, which fell from #20 to #21.

As I’ve noted in years past, RPL correlates generally with prestige. Of the top 20 firms in RPL, 14 are in the Vault 20. These are the six firms that are top 20 in RPL but not in the Vault 20: Paul Hastings (#12 in RPL, #21 in Vault); Dechert (#16 in RPL, #51 in Vault); Fenwick & West (#17 in RPL, #71 in Vault); King & Spalding (#18 in RPL, #36 in Vault); Proskauer Rose (#19 in RPL, #33 in Vault); and Debevoise (#20 in RPL, #23 in Vault).

And of the Vault 20 firms, 14 are in the top 20 in RPL. These are the six Vault 20 firms that are not in the top 20 by RPL: Sidley (#11 in Vault, #21 in RPL); Covington & Burling (#12 in Vault, #36 in RPL); Weil Gotshal (#15 in Vault, #27 in RPL); Cleary Gottlieb (#16 in Vault, #25 in RPL); Williams & Connolly (#17 in Vault, #29 in RPL); and White & Case (#18 in Vault, #47 in RPL).

As you’d expect, high RPL correlates not only with prestige, but with profitability. There’s a lot of overlap between the top 20 firms in RPL and the top 20 firms in profits per equity partner (listed below). There are only three firms in the top 20 by RPL that are not in the top 20 by PEP: Susman (#2 in RPL, #22 in PEP); Fenwick (#17 in RPL, #31 in PEP); and Proskauer (#19 in RPL, #24 in PEP).

As for the three firms that are in the top 20 by PEP but not by RPL, those would be Cleary (#17 in PEP, #25 in RPL); Sidley (#19 in PEP, #21 in RPL); and Weil (#20 in PEP, #27 in RPL).

Reflecting the relative difficulty of growing RPL (compared to gross revenue or profits per equity partner), only four firms in the Am Law 100 grew RPL by more than 20%: Littler Mendelson (33.73%), Shook Hardy (24.12%), Quinn Emanuel (23.39%), and Susman (20.39%).

Three firms experienced dips in RPL: Gordon Rees (-15.79%), Cadwalader (-0.07%), and… Cravath (-0.04%). It’s not much of a drop, but having basically flat RPL isn’t a great place for Cravath to be—and combined with the recent partner departures, it makes me wonder if it’s not the firm it once was.

Profits Per Equity Partner

And now, what you’ve all been waiting for: the top 20 firms in the 2026 Am Law 100 ranking of profits per equity partner, based on PEP in 2025. You can access the full list at The American Lawyer.

Fueled by a robust market for big-ticket, public M&A, Wachtell Lipton, the #1 firm, grew profits per equity partner by 34.48%, to $12.152 million. Wresting its PEP crown back from K&E, WLRK set a new record for profits per equity partner, becoming the first Am Law 100 firm to break the $12 million mark.

It might have dropped to #2, but Kirkland had a great year as well: growing PEP by a healthy 20.19%, it hit $11.121 million.

In 2025, a remarkable 15 firms broke the $7 million mark in PEP—up from eight in 2024 and three in 2023—and an impressive 17 firms out of the top 20 grew PEP by double digits.

The top 10 firms were almost entirely the same, just switching places within the group, except that Paul Hastings (#9) replaced Cravath (#13).

The top 20 firms were also almost entirely the same firms, just reshuffling internally, except Fried Frank (#21) was replaced by Dechert (#16).

For this year’s ranking, it took $5.593 million in PEP to make the top 20—up significantly from the $4.989 million it took for last year’s ranking.

What’s the deal with the “N/A” for Ropes & Gray? As explained in this article, “For the first time, Law.com will report a nonequity tier for Ropes & Gray’s most recent financial results, consistent with the Am Law 100 methodology for nonequity partners. As a result of the change in reporting the firm’s partnership structure, year-over-year percentage changes for Ropes’ net income, profits per equity partners and average compensation per partner are not available.”

With these PEP figures in mind, let’s return to the topic of partner compensation. PEP is an average, so at every firm, some partners earn more and some earn less. The gap between the highest- and lowest-paid equity partners is captured by a concept called the “spread”: if the spread is 3-to-1, the highest-paid partner earns three times as much as the lowest-paid partner, whereas if the spread is 20-to-1, the highest-paid partner earns 20 times as much as the lowest-paid partner.

Back in the heyday of lockstep compensation, when partners were paid based on seniority, spreads tended to be narrow or low. For example, back in 2007, Cravath and Cleary had spreads of 3:1. Cravath’s PEP back then was around $3 million—so you could imagine a lockstep scale in which the most junior partners made $1.5 million, midlevel partners made $3 million, and the most senior partners made $4.5 million.

But with lockstep compensation largely a thing of the past, replaced by so-called “eat what you kill” compensation—or, to be more genteel, “merit-based” compensation—spreads today are dramatically higher. I don’t know the highest spread out there today—please drop me a line if you think your firm has a particularly high one—but I can say that spreads of 15:1 or even 20:1 are no longer unheard of.

Imagine a firm with PEP of $7 million—of which there are 15, per Am Law. It might have the lowest-paid equity partner making $1.5 million, and the highest-paid equity partner making $30 million—for a spread of 20:1. Or take a firm with PEP of $8 million, of which there are nine. It might have the lowest-paid equity partner making $2 million, and the highest-paid equity partner making $40 million.

Because the partners who make $1.5 million or $2 million vastly outnumber the partners who make $30 million or $40 million, it’s not hard to get the math to work with PEP of $7 million or $8 million. Yes, the mean in terms of partner comp is still $7 million or $8 million, but the distribution is far from a bell curve. Instead, it’s heavily weighted toward the low end of the curve—and even the modal partner pay might be below $7 million or $8 million. All the partners earning less than the average are making possible the eye-popping pay of the highest-paid partners.

I expect to have more to say about this in future posts, including a discussion of the rules of thumb about how much a partner should be paid based on their book of business (which suggest that certain partners might be undercompensated at $30 million or $40 million). For now, suffice it to say that based on the latest profits per partner data from the Am Law 100—combined with other trends, including ever-increasing billing rates, ballooning books of business, growing nonequity tiers, rising leverage, and astronomically larger partner-pay spreads—I can confirm that eight-figure partner paydays are both real and spectacular.

Ed. note: If you’re a litigation partner at Am Law 200 firm or leading boutique who’s interested in learning more about increasing your firm’s profitability by handling plaintiff-side work for corporate clients, please join us for our upcoming Burford Briefing breakfast panel, “Biglaw’s Embrace of Contingency Litigation,” on May 6 in New York City. To learn more and register your interest, please visit the event page.

Earlier:

This post is only for paid subscribers of Original Jurisdiction . As always, thanks for your support!

item.title: Judicial Notice (04.26.26): The GOAT of SCOTUS Advocates?
item.description: BriefCatch is trusted by over 22,000 legal professionals, 40 Am Law 200 firms, and 70+ courts. Built directly into Microsoft Word, it delivers real-time insights to help you write with precision, clarity, and confidence. And with RealityCheck, BriefCatch now verifies that citations are accurate and…
item.author: David Lat
item.content:encoded:
BriefCatch is trusted by over 22,000 legal professionals, 40 Am Law 200 firms, and 70+ courts. Built directly into Microsoft Word, it delivers real-time insights to help you write with precision, clarity, and confidence. And with RealityCheck, BriefCatch now verifies that citations are accurate and authorities are used correctly. To learn more, schedule a meeting with an expert—or try it out for free with BriefCatch Express.

Last week was uneventful for me, mainly spent dealing with domestic matters. But I did work on a detailed outline for the panel I’ll be moderating in Manhattan on May 6, focused on Biglaw’s move into plaintiff-side litigation.

Why the need for a detailed outline? Attendees can now receive Continuing Legal Education (CLE) credit for New York, New Jersey, or Pennsylvania. For details and to express interest in attending, please check out the event page. Free breakfast and CLE credit—what’s not to like? Thanks to my friends at Burford Capital, for hosting what should be a great event, and Lawline, for making the CLE possible.

Now let’s turn to the news—of which there was a ridiculous amount, making the threshold for getting mentioned higher than usual. If I fail to discuss something newsworthy in this edition of Judicial Notice—or if you find my treatment of any development to be lacking in any respect, because I had to go shorter than usual to cover everything I wanted to cover—please add your thoughts in the comments.

I wrote that last paragraph before the Saturday night exchange of gunfire at the White House Correspondents’ Dinner, attended by Donald Trump and several of his Cabinet members—who were rushed to safety and unharmed. A 31-year-old man from California, Cole Tomas Allen, was taken into custody. Cole will be charged with federal crimes, including using a firearm during a crime of violence and assault on a federal officer using a dangerous weapon. (A Secret Service agent was shot, but he was wearing a bulletproof vest and survived.)

Lawyer of the Week: Paul Clement.

In a little over two weeks, the D.C. Circuit will hear oral argument in the Trump administration’s appeal of its four losses in the Biglaw executive-order cases. Because the cases were consolidated for argument, only one lawyer can appear for the firms. Who would it be? The competition was stiff: the lead attorneys for the four firms consisted of three former U.S. solicitors general—Paul Clement of Clement & Murphy, Elizabeth Prelogar of Cooley, Donald Verrilli of Munger Tolles & Olson—and Dane Butswinkas, a leading litigator and former chair of Williams & Connolly.

Who did the law firms choose? I don’t know what process they employed—but as reported by Bloomberg Law and Reuters, they’ll be represented by Clement, one of the best appellate advocates in history. Meanwhile, the Department of Justice lawyer with the unenviable task of going up against Clement is Deputy Associate Attorney General Abhishek Kambli (profiled by Justin Henry of Bloomberg.)

Paul Clement is a busy man. Tomorrow, he’ll argue before the Supreme Court in Monsanto Company v. Durnell, a major case about federal preemption—with billions of dollars on the line.

For more about Clement, listen to our podcast conversation—he was my second guest, because I wanted to kick off my podcast with big names—or read my prior post, 3 Tips For Appellate Advocates—From Paul Clement.

Other lawyers in the news:

Speaking of boutique founding partners and former podcast guests, Steve Molo of MoloLamken also has a lot on his plate. He represents Elon Musk in the gazillionaire’s lawsuit against Sam Altman over the origins of OpenAI, which is about to be tried before Judge Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers (N.D. Cal.). And lawyers in a major financial aid price-fixing case have asked for Molo and his firm to be brought into the case, to “ensure that the proposed class has excellent representation” (after another firm was removed for ethical issues).

What’s the latest in the horse race to lead the DOJ as attorney general? Right now, Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche appears to be the frontrunner, and the job is his “to win or lose,” according to The New York Times (gift link). One potential rival, U.S. Attorney Jeanine Pirro (D.D.C.), apparently isn’t interested. Another possible contender, Civil Rights Division leader Harmeet Dhillon, has told Blanche she’s a “team player” who wants to help him succeed—and might be more interested in the #3 job at the DOJ, associate attorney general.

James Percival, general counsel of the Department of Homeland Security, spoke at UCLA Law School—where he was the subject of a rowdy protest by more than 150 demonstrators. The Foundation for Individual Rights and Expression (FIRE), an organization committed to defending free speech, explained that “[s]tudents have every right to object, question, and peacefully protest invited speakers. But sustained disruption preventing others from hearing a speaker has no place at a university committed to free inquiry.”

In other news related to the top lawyer of a federal agency, Pierre Gentin is stepping down as general counsel of the Commerce Department. A former CLO of McKinsey and partner at Cahill Gordon, Gentin told me he hasn’t settled on what he plans to do next, other than travel and spend some time with his wife, lawyer turned novelist Reyna Gentin (whose latest novel, Jessica Harmon Has Stepped Away, was published a few months ago).

In another speech-related controversy, Berkeley Law’s chapter of Students for Justice in Palestine received criticism for hosting an event featuring video remarks by Israa Jaabis, a Palestinian woman who was convicted of detonating a car bomb in Israel in 2015. Jaabis was released from prison in November 2023, in exchange for hostages kidnapped by Hamas on October 7 of that year.

In other news from the Golden State, here’s an interesting profile of Bill Essayli—who’s currently leading the U.S. Attorney’s Office in Los Angeles (C.D. Cal.), as First Assistant U.S. Attorney—by Matt Hamilton for L.A. Material.

Yikes: Jeanne Christensen, a partner at Wigdor and prominent plaintiff-side employment litigator, was sanctioned by Judge Jessica Clarke (S.D.N.Y.). In her order, Judge Clarke wrote that Christensen “lied repeatedly to the Court and to opposing counsel in this litigation about what was happening in a related action.”

In memoriam:

Sonia Pressmen Fuentes—an early women’s rights lawyer, and the first female attorney in the general counsel’s office at the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission—passed away at 97.

George Ariyoshi—Hawaii’s (and America’s) first governor of Asian descent, who earned his law degree from the University of Michigan and worked in private practice before and after his political career—passed away at 100.

May they rest in peace.

Judge of the Week: Judge Alan Albright.

On Tuesday, Judge Alan Albright (W.D. Tex.), 67, announced that he’s stepping down at the end of August. The news was covered by outlets including Bloomberg Law (which broke the story), Law.com, Law360, and Reuters—a lot of media coverage, especially for a district judge. But Judge Albright is no ordinary district judge.

A former IP litigator, Albright was appointed to the bench in 2018 by Donald Trump. By 2021, Albright was handling around 25 percent of all federal patent cases, turning his Waco courthouse into a national hub for IP litigation. How did this happen? Judge shopping: Albright was the only district judge in the Waco Division of the Western District of Texas, so filing in Waco guaranteed your case went to him.

As a result, plaintiffs flocked to Waco—perhaps because of Albright’s expertise in patent law, perhaps because he was friendly to plaintiffs, or perhaps because he moved cases quickly (which tends to be good for plaintiffs), depending on whom you asked. After complaints about this arrangement, then-Chief Judge Orlando Garcia changed assignment procedures to require any new patent cases filed in Waco to be randomly assigned among the district’s dozen or so judges, which brought down Albright’s patent docket. (For pushback on claims that Albright was “plaintiff-friendly,” see this LinkedIn post by commercial and IP litigator Michael C. Smith.)

Where is Albright headed? He didn’t announce his destination, but news reports claim that he’s returning to private practice—which makes sense. He’s resigning rather than retiring—i.e., he’s not taking senior status, because he’s not eligible yet—so his new opportunity is presumably lucrative enough to make up for not getting his salary for life (a nice benefit of federal judicial service for judges who take senior status). Good luck to Judge Albright in his next endeavor.

In other news about judges and the judiciary:

Last week, I mentioned leaked memos (gift link) relating to the Supreme Court’s emergency/shadow/interim docket, which were published by Jodi Kantor and Adam Liptak in The Times (gift link). The so-called “Shadow Papers” provoked the equivalent of a WWE “battle royale” among law professors and legal commentators—a fascinating debate, but way too meaty for me to delve into here. To read some of the takes, see footnote 4 of my recent post, When Justices Stop Being Polite—And Start Getting Real (updated to include additional commentary from Steve Vladeck, Chris Geidner, and Will Baude).

In remarks on Tuesday at Harvard University, retired Justice Stephen Breyer weighed in on the emergency docket. He acknowledged the “danger” involved in the Court deciding too many issues with too little reasoning—but he also said, in defense of his former colleagues, “I do not believe that the members of the Supreme Court are there to carry out some political agenda.”

Speaking of SCOTUS, former law clerks and retired judges submitted amicus briefs urging the high court to review Judge Pauline Newman’s (thus far unsuccessful) challenge to her suspension from the Federal Circuit.

Human judges can be influenced by their political priors—as critics of the shadow docket allege. Human judges get old—like Judge Newman, who will turn 99 in June. Would we be better off with AI courts? Writing in The Nation, Elie Mystal makes the case against robot judges.

In nominations news, the Senate confirmed Andrew Davis, a partner at Lehotsky Keller Cohn, to a district-court seat (W.D. Tex.).

Job of the Week: an opportunity for a real estate associate in Dallas.

Lateral Link is recruiting a junior to midlevel real estate associate (2–6 years) for a prestigious Am Law firm in Dallas. The role covers sophisticated commercial real estate work—acquisitions/dispositions, joint ventures, development projects, and financing transactions—with responsibility for drafting and negotiating PSAs, JV agreements, and loan documents (acquisition, construction, mezzanine, refinancing). Expect meaningful client interaction with institutional investors, developers, and lenders; strong mentorship; a collaborative culture; steady deal flow; and top-of-market compensation. If you’re even slightly curious, please email Wendy Boone at wendyboone@laterallink.com for a confidential conversation.

Ruling of the Week: Nathan v. Alamo Heights Independent School District .

The U.S. Supreme Court issued three decisions last week—but to paraphrase Obi-Wan Kenobi, these weren’t the decisions you were looking for. There were two rulings in argued cases, Hencely v. Fluor Corp. (preemption) and

So let’s talk about a case that is surely on its way to SCOTUS: Nathan v. Alamo Heights Independent School District. The Fifth Circuit, sitting en banc, rejected Establishment and Free Exercise Clause challenges to a Texas law, SB 10, that requires public schools to display a copy of the Ten Commandments in classrooms. For collected coverage of the decision, see Howard Bashman’s

The vote was 9-8. Judge Stuart Kyle Duncan wrote the majority opinion—and knowing that not everyone would read his 53-page opinion, he helpfully included a five-page introduction offering a concise summary of his reasoning. He argued that Stone v. Graham (1980), in which the Supreme Court invalidated a similar Kentucky law, was no longer good law, after the high court’s rejection of the so-called “Lemon test” for finding an Establishment Clause violation in Kennedy v. Bremerton School District (2022). He wrote that “[i]n place of Lemon, courts now ask a question rooted in the past: does the law at issue resemble a founding-era religious establishment?” And under the new framework, Judge Duncan contended, SB 10 passes muster:

SB 10 looks nothing like a historical religious establishment. It does not tell churches or synagogues or mosques what to believe or how to worship or whom to employ as priests, rabbis, or imams. It punishes no one who rejects the Ten Commandments, no matter the reason. It levies no taxes to support any clergy. It does not co-opt churches to perform civic functions. These are the kinds of things “establishments of religion” did at the founding. SB 10 does none of them.

Eight judges dissented, joining different portions of four separate dissents. In general, the dissenters focused heavily on how on-point Stone v. Graham was, arguing that it survived the abandonment of Lemon—and that if Stone is no longer good law, it’s for the Supreme Court to say so, not the Fifth Circuit.

Other noteworthy decisions and dispositions:

United States v. Texas. The Ten Commandments ruling wasn’t the only high-profile decision from the en banc Fifth Circuit last week. In an opinion by Judge

Refugee and Immigrant Center for Education and Legal Services v. Mullin. In an opinion by Judge

United States v. California. In an opinion by Judge

Ream v. U.S. Department of the Treasury. In an opinion by Judge

United States v. Keirans. Here is John Ross’s summary for

United States v. Alsenat. In an opinion by Judge

Moore v. Senate Majority PAC. In an opinion by Judge

Staying on defamation but turning to the trial courts, it wasn’t a great week for plaintiffs:

Judge George C. Hanks Jr. (S.D. Tex.) dismissed FBI Director Kash Patel’s defamation lawsuit against former FBI official Frank Figliuzzi, who said on air, on Morning Joe, that Patel “has been visible at nightclubs far more than he has been on the seventh floor of the Hoover building.”

Judge James Moody Jr. (M.D. Fla.) dismissed right-wing influencer Laura Loomer’s defamation lawsuit against Bill Maher and HBO. Loomer sued over Maher’s joke that she “might be” in a sexual relationship with Trump.

Well-known New York litigator Marc Kasowitz withdrew from the team defending right-wing podcaster Candace Owens against defamation claims filed by French President Emmanuel Macron and his wife, Brigitte.

In North Carolina, Uber lost a second bellwether trial arising out of a rider’s allegation that she was sexually assaulted by a driver she booked through the app. But the jury awarded the plaintiff only $5,000—a far cry from the $8.5 million that an Arizona jury awarded in the first trial earlier this year.

Litigation of the Week: United States v. Southern Poverty Law Center, Inc.

On Tuesday, the Justice Department charged the Southern Poverty Law Center, a civil rights group, with financial crimes, arising out of the SPLC’s former practice of using donor money to covertly pay informants inside extremist organizations. According to the DOJ, “[b]etween 2014 and 2023, the SPLC secretly funneled more than $3 million in donated funds to individuals who were associated with various violent extremist groups including the [KKK], Aryan Nations, and National Socialist Party of America.”

The 14-page indictment charges the SPLC with six counts of wire fraud, four counts of making false statements to a federally insured bank, and one count of conspiracy to commit concealment money laundering. The wire fraud counts focus on the alleged defrauding of donors, while the false statement counts are based on the SPLC opening bank accounts in the names of non-existent businesses (accounts that it used to make the payments, according to the indictment).

Most reactions to the indictment were negative, with legal experts criticizing both the strength of the government case and the decision to pursue an organization that is politically adverse to Trump. But responses varied based on political viewpoint; as Professor Jed Rubenfeld summed up, critics viewed the indictment “as yet another vindictive Trump prosecution of a left-wing adversary,” while defenders saw it as “overdue justice finally coming to a radical left nonprofit” that went around “call[ing] right-leaning Catholic organizations domestic terrorists.”

The conservative editorial board of The Wall Street Journal (gift link) offered a mixed assessment. It argued that “Trump’s lawfare against his political opponents is destructive, but that doesn’t mean every case is unjustified,” and it criticized the SPLC for defining extremist or hate groups “to include mainstream and nonthreatening conservative groups like the Alliance Defending Freedom.” In the WSJ’s view, “To the extent the money [paid to informants] encouraged or sustained the racist groups, tacitly or otherwise, SPLC benefited from perpetuating racial division. A court will decide if that’s illegal, but it’s certainly disreputable.”

In other litigation news:

U.S. Attorney Jeanine Pirro (D.D.C.) announced that the DOJ had closed its investigation into Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell—possibly to facilitate the confirmation of Kevin Warsh, nominated by Trump to replace Powell as chair. Senator Thom Tillis (R-N.C.) said he would oppose the confirmation of Warsh until the “bogus” investigation into Powell was resolved.

The Supreme Court granted certiorari in St. Mary Catholic Parish v. Roy, which challenges the exclusion of Catholic preschools in Colorado from the state’s universal preschool program because the schools won’t admit LGBTQ children or children with LGBTQ parents.

The Justice Department issued a report calling for an expansion of the federal death penalty, including recommendations to add firing squads, electrocution, and lethal gas as possible methods of execution.

The DOJ is also targeting 384 foreign-born Americans for denaturalization, alleging that they obtained their U.S. citizenship through fraud.

FBI Director Kash Patel sued The Atlantic and one of its reporters for defamation over an article that claimed FBI and DOJ officials were “alarmed” by Patel’s “conspicuous inebriation and unexplained absences.”

The administration reached a settlement with 2016 Trump campaign adviser Carter Page, who had filed a lawsuit alleging that he was subjected to unlawful surveillance starting in 2016, during the Obama administration.

Deal of the Week: SpaceX acquiring the right to buy the AI coding startup Cursor for $60 billion.

One difference between the dot-com and AI booms is that many AI companies have real products and revenue. Take Cursor, an AI startup founded by four MIT graduates in 2022, which already generates $100 million in annual recurring revenue from its popular coding tools. As reported by The Times and the WSJ, Cursor just struck a deal with Elon Musk’s SpaceX—which could be worth $10 billion, or $60 billion.

What explains the “choose your own adventure” nature of this transaction? According to SpaceX, the companies “are now working closely together to create the world’s best coding and knowledge-work AI.” Under the terms of their agreement, SpaceX has the right to acquire Cursor later this year for $60 billion—but if SpaceX doesn’t exercise that option, it will pay Cursor $10 billion for their work together.

The law firms handling this big-ticket, complex deal are Kirkland & Ellis for Cursor and Gibson Dunn & Crutcher for SpaceX. Gibson knows SpaceX well: partner Hillary Holmes heads up the GDC team handling SpaceX’s IPO, expected to happen later this year and value the company at around $2 trillion—a record-setting sum.

Law Firm of the Week: Sullivan & Cromwell.

Last week wasn’t great for Sullivan & Cromwell. First, as previously reported, S&C had to apologize to Chief Judge Martin Glenn of U.S. Bankruptcy Court (S.D.N.Y.) for filing a motion riddled with AI-induced errors. This makes S&C the most prestigious firm to date to ‘fess up to an AI problem. (Speaking of problems, I apologize for the many typos in the original version of my story—which I blame not on AI but on writing in a hurry, before our child care timed out, with kids already climbing all over me.)

Second—and more significantly, even if “S&C AI Fail” stories get more clicks—SullCrom lost a quartet of appellate and Supreme Court superstars. As reported by C. Ryan Barber and Erin Mulvaney of the WSJ (gift link), former acting solicitor general Jeffrey Wall and three fellow former SCOTUS clerks and alumni of the SG’s office—Morgan Ratner, Judson (Judd) Littleton, and Yaira Dubin—took their talents to Gibson Dunn. Per the Journal, Wall is receiving a partnership share expected to be worth more than $15 million annually.

As I observed on X, “this move makes sense: Gibson is one of a shrinking group of Biglaw firms that still cares about litigation (as GDC partner Orin Snyder told me in our podcast interview).” The S&C litigators will add to Gibson’s already strong appellate group, including three current practice leaders—Tom Dupree, Allyson Ho, and Julian Poon (Jeff Wall is the fourth)—as well as Chambers-ranked stars like Ted Boutrous, Miguel Estrada, Theane Evangelis, Tom Hungar, and Helgi Walker. (Kudos to Boutrous on last week’s nice Sixth Circuit en banc win—in a complex case about class certification in the insurance context, so I’ll spare you the details.)

In my X thread, I wondered whether S&C, a top transactional firm, would try to find new appellate talent—or whether it would simply “double down on closing gazillion-dollar M&A deals.” According to Patrick Smith of Am Law, Sullivan is doing the former—and is “well on the way to filling the void” left by Wall et al. It will be interesting to see whom they hire, especially in light of the talent already off the market (as discussed in Move of the Week below).

In other firm news:

I previously discussed DLA Piper’s plan to abandon the verein structure, noting that it was subject to a partnership vote. The vote took place—and the partners approved the proposal, with the new structure taking effect on May 1.

A defunct tech company is suing Norton Rose Fulbright and a former associate for malpractice and other claims, seeking $100 million in damages.

Speaking of lawsuits against Biglaw firms, an anonymous 38-year-old Black female associate is suing McDermott Will & Schulte, alleging discrimination—she was repeatedly passed over for partner—and retaliation.

There’s no litigation yet, but Seattle-based litigation counsel Breanna Philips claims that a (female) former partner at Perkins Coie harassed her—and the firm has hired an outside investigator to examine her allegations.

Move of the Week: Kannon Shanmugam joining Davis Polk from Paul Weiss.

One of the very first interviews I conducted for OJ was when I caught up with star SCOTUS advocate Kannon Shanmugam, shortly after his 30th oral argument before the Supreme Court in December 2020. Five years later, he’s closing in on his 40th argument before the Court—but he’s no longer at Paul Weiss, his professional home at the time of our conversation.

Shanmugam and another Paul Weiss partner, Masha Hansford, moved to Davis Polk, where they will launch a Supreme Court and appellate practice group. The move was covered not only in legal outlets like Bloomberg Law and Law360, but by more general-interest publications such as The Times (gift link) and the WSJ (gift link)—which noted that star SCOTUS litigators are securing eight-figure pay packages.

How much did Shanmugam receive to move to DPW? This wasn’t disclosed, but if I had to guess, I’d say between $18 million and $20 million. That might seem like a ton of money—and, of course, it is—but it’s not at the top of the Biglaw pay scale. According to legal recruiters interviewed by Law360, some partners are receiving as much as $40 million. In the words of Gloria Sandrino, co-chair of the partner practice at Lateral Link, the figure of profits per equity partner is “no longer a reliable indicator of a firm’s true compensation capacity,” since firms today are willing to tolerate a higher spread between their lowest- and highest-paid partners. As Sandrino put it, the notion of trying to keep the spread below a certain number—say, 20-to-1, which was once viewed as corrosive to firm culture—is “out the door now.”

Other notable moves:

The movement in the appellate space is off the charts. In addition to the S&C-to-GDC and PW-to-DPW moves discussed above, Kirkland & Ellis hired Matt Rice, the outgoing solicitor general of Tennessee.

Speaking of state SGs, down in Atlanta, Jones Day—another firm still committed to litigation—added Stephen Petrany, who returned to JD after four years serving as Georgia solicitor general.

Almost a year after its founding by a trio of top trial lawyers who left Paul Weiss, the rapidly growing Dunn Isaacson Rhee welcomed two partners from Latham & Watkins in D.C.: Danielle Conley and Jude Volek, who both served in the White House Counsel’s Office in the Biden Administration.

Even after losing litigators like Kannon Shanmugam and the DIR founders, Paul Weiss isn’t giving up on litigation—reflected in its hiring of Chicago trial lawyer Adam Hoeflich, who was recruited away from Bartlit Beck by PW chair Scott Barshay (a friend dating back to college). Hoeflich will work out of New York.

Speaking of the Big Apple, Holtzman Vogel is opening a Manhattan office—and the firm hired litigation partner Akiva Shapiro from Gibson Dunn to lead it.

And that’s all for this action-packed edition of Judicial Notice. Thanks for joining me, and I wish you a great week ahead.

This post is only for paid subscribers of Original Jurisdiction . As always, thanks for your support!

item.title: Judicial Notice (04.26.26): The GOAT of SCOTUS Advocates?
item.description: BriefCatch is trusted by over 22,000 legal professionals, 40 Am Law 200 firms, and 70+ courts. And with RealityCheck, BriefCatch now verifies that citations are accurate and…
item.author: David Lat
item.content:encoded:
BriefCatch is trusted by over 22,000 legal professionals, 40 Am Law 200 firms, and 70+ courts. Built directly into Microsoft Word, it delivers real-time insights to help you write with precision, clarity, and confidence. And with RealityCheck, BriefCatch now verifies that citations are accurate and authorities are used correctly. To learn more, schedule a meeting with an expert—or try it out for free with BriefCatch Express.

Last week was uneventful for me, mainly spent dealing with domestic matters. But I did work on a detailed outline for the panel I’ll be moderating in Manhattan on May 6, focused on Biglaw’s move into plaintiff-side litigation.

Why the need for a detailed outline? Attendees can now receive Continuing Legal Education (CLE) credit for New York, New Jersey, or Pennsylvania. For details and to express interest in attending, please check out the event page. Free breakfast and CLE credit—what’s not to like? Thanks to my friends at Burford Capital, for hosting what should be a great event, and Lawline, for making the CLE possible.

Now let’s turn to the news—of which there was a ridiculous amount, making the threshold for getting mentioned higher than usual. If I fail to discuss something newsworthy in this edition of Judicial Notice—or if you find my treatment of any development to be lacking in any respect, because I had to go shorter than usual to cover everything I wanted to cover—please add your thoughts in the comments.

I wrote that last paragraph before the Saturday night exchange of gunfire at the White House Correspondents’ Dinner, attended by Donald Trump and several of his Cabinet members—who were rushed to safety and unharmed. A 31-year-old man from California, Cole Tomas Allen, was taken into custody. Cole will be charged with federal crimes, including using a firearm during a crime of violence and assault on a federal officer using a dangerous weapon. (A Secret Service agent was shot, but he was wearing a bulletproof vest and survived.)

Lawyer of the Week: Paul Clement.

In a little over two weeks, the D.C. Circuit will hear oral argument in the Trump administration’s appeal of its four losses in the Biglaw executive-order cases. Because the cases were consolidated for argument, only one lawyer can appear for the firms. Who would it be? The competition was stiff: the lead attorneys for the four firms consisted of three former U.S. solicitors general—Paul Clement of Clement & Murphy, Elizabeth Prelogar of Cooley, Donald Verrilli of Munger Tolles & Olson—and Dane Butswinkas, a leading litigator and former chair of Williams & Connolly.

Who did the law firms choose? I don’t know what process they employed—but as reported by Bloomberg Law and Reuters, they’ll be represented by Clement, one of the best appellate advocates in history. Meanwhile, the Department of Justice lawyer with the unenviable task of going up against Clement is Deputy Associate Attorney General Abhishek Kambli (profiled by Justin Henry of Bloomberg.)

Paul Clement is a busy man. Tomorrow, he’ll argue before the Supreme Court in Monsanto Company v. Durnell, a major case about federal preemption—with billions of dollars on the line.

For more about Clement, listen to our podcast conversation—he was my second guest, because I wanted to kick off my podcast with big names—or read my prior post, 3 Tips For Appellate Advocates—From Paul Clement.

Other lawyers in the news:

Speaking of boutique founding partners and former podcast guests, Steve Molo of MoloLamken also has a lot on his plate. He represents Elon Musk in the gazillionaire’s lawsuit against Sam Altman over the origins of OpenAI, which is about to be tried before Judge Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers (N.D. Cal.). And lawyers in a major financial aid price-fixing case have asked for Molo and his firm to be brought into the case, to “ensure that the proposed class has excellent representation” (after another firm was removed for ethical issues).

What’s the latest in the horse race to lead the DOJ as attorney general? Right now, Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche appears to be the frontrunner, and the job is his “to win or lose,” according to The New York Times (gift link). One potential rival, U.S. Attorney Jeanine Pirro (D.D.C.), apparently isn’t interested. Another possible contender, Civil Rights Division leader Harmeet Dhillon, has told Blanche she’s a “team player” who wants to help him succeed—and might be more interested in the #3 job at the DOJ, associate attorney general.

James Percival, general counsel of the Department of Homeland Security, spoke at UCLA Law School—where he was the subject of a rowdy protest by more than 150 demonstrators. The Foundation for Individual Rights and Expression (FIRE), an organization committed to defending free speech, explained that “[s]tudents have every right to object, question, and peacefully protest invited speakers. But sustained disruption preventing others from hearing a speaker has no place at a university committed to free inquiry.”

In other news related to the top lawyer of a federal agency, Pierre Gentin is stepping down as general counsel of the Commerce Department. A former CLO of McKinsey and partner at Cahill Gordon, Gentin told me he hasn’t settled on what he plans to do next, other than travel and spend some time with his wife, lawyer turned novelist Reyna Gentin (whose latest novel, Jessica Harmon Has Stepped Away, was published a few months ago).

In another speech-related controversy, Berkeley Law’s chapter of Students for Justice in Palestine received criticism for hosting an event featuring video remarks by Israa Jaabis, a Palestinian woman who was convicted of detonating a car bomb in Israel in 2015. Jaabis was released from prison in November 2023, in exchange for hostages kidnapped by Hamas on October 7 of that year.

In other news from the Golden State, here’s an interesting profile of Bill Essayli—who’s currently leading the U.S. Attorney’s Office in Los Angeles (C.D. Cal.), as First Assistant U.S. Attorney—by Matt Hamilton for L.A. Material.

Yikes: Jeanne Christensen, a partner at Wigdor and prominent plaintiff-side employment litigator, was sanctioned by Judge Jessica Clarke (S.D.N.Y.). In her order, Judge Clarke wrote that Christensen “lied repeatedly to the Court and to opposing counsel in this litigation about what was happening in a related action.”

In memoriam:

Sonia Pressmen Fuentes—an early women’s rights lawyer, and the first female attorney in the general counsel’s office at the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission—passed away at 97.

George Ariyoshi—Hawaii’s (and America’s) first governor of Asian descent, who earned his law degree from the University of Michigan and worked in private practice before and after his political career—passed away at 100.

May they rest in peace.

Judge of the Week: Judge Alan Albright.

On Tuesday, Judge Alan Albright (W.D. Tex.), 67, announced that he’s stepping down at the end of August. The news was covered by outlets including Bloomberg Law (which broke the story), Law.com, Law360, and Reuters—a lot of media coverage, especially for a district judge. But Judge Albright is no ordinary district judge.

A former IP litigator, Albright was appointed to the bench in 2018 by Donald Trump. By 2021, Albright was handling around 25 percent of all federal patent cases, turning his Waco courthouse into a national hub for IP litigation. How did this happen? Judge shopping: Albright was the only district judge in the Waco Division of the Western District of Texas, so filing in Waco guaranteed your case went to him.

As a result, plaintiffs flocked to Waco—perhaps because of Albright’s expertise in patent law, perhaps because he was friendly to plaintiffs, or perhaps because he moved cases quickly (which tends to be good for plaintiffs), depending on whom you asked. After complaints about this arrangement, then-Chief Judge Orlando Garcia changed assignment procedures to require any new patent cases filed in Waco to be randomly assigned among the district’s dozen or so judges, which brought down Albright’s patent docket. (For pushback on claims that Albright was “plaintiff-friendly,” see this LinkedIn post by commercial and IP litigator Michael C. Smith.)

Where is Albright headed? He didn’t announce his destination, but news reports claim that he’s returning to private practice—which makes sense. He’s resigning rather than retiring—i.e., he’s not taking senior status, because he’s not eligible yet—so his new opportunity is presumably lucrative enough to make up for not getting his salary for life (a nice benefit of federal judicial service for judges who take senior status). Good luck to Judge Albright in his next endeavor.

In other news about judges and the judiciary:

Last week, I mentioned leaked memos (gift link) relating to the Supreme Court’s emergency/shadow/interim docket, which were published by Jodi Kantor and Adam Liptak in The Times (gift link). The so-called “Shadow Papers” provoked the equivalent of a WWE “battle royale” among law professors and legal commentators—a fascinating debate, but way too meaty for me to delve into here. To read some of the takes, see footnote 4 of my recent post, When Justices Stop Being Polite—And Start Getting Real (updated to include additional commentary from Steve Vladeck, Chris Geidner, and Will Baude).

In remarks on Tuesday at Harvard University, retired Justice Stephen Breyer weighed in on the emergency docket. He acknowledged the “danger” involved in the Court deciding too many issues with too little reasoning—but he also said, in defense of his former colleagues, “I do not believe that the members of the Supreme Court are there to carry out some political agenda.”

Speaking of SCOTUS, former law clerks and retired judges submitted amicus briefs urging the high court to review Judge Pauline Newman’s (thus far unsuccessful) challenge to her suspension from the Federal Circuit.

Human judges can be influenced by their political priors—as critics of the shadow docket allege. Human judges get old—like Judge Newman, who will turn 99 in June. Would we be better off with AI courts? Writing in The Nation, Elie Mystal makes the case against robot judges.

In nominations news, the Senate confirmed Andrew Davis, a partner at Lehotsky Keller Cohn, to a district-court seat (W.D. Tex.).

Job of the Week: an opportunity for a real estate associate in Dallas.

Lateral Link is recruiting a junior to midlevel real estate associate (2–6 years) for a prestigious Am Law firm in Dallas. The role covers sophisticated commercial real estate work—acquisitions/dispositions, joint ventures, development projects, and financing transactions—with responsibility for drafting and negotiating PSAs, JV agreements, and loan documents (acquisition, construction, mezzanine, refinancing). Expect meaningful client interaction with institutional investors, developers, and lenders; strong mentorship; a collaborative culture; steady deal flow; and top-of-market compensation. If you’re even slightly curious, please email Wendy Boone at wendyboone@laterallink.com for a confidential conversation.

The U.S. Supreme Court issued three decisions last week—but to paraphrase Obi-Wan Kenobi, these weren’t the decisions you were looking for. There were two rulings in argued cases, Hencely v. Fluor Corp. (preemption) and

Bremerton School District (2022). He wrote that “[i]n place of Lemon, courts now ask a question rooted in the past: does the law at issue resemble a founding-era religious establishment?” And under the new framework, Judge Duncan contended, SB 10 passes muster:

SB 10 looks nothing like a historical religious establishment. It does not tell churches or synagogues or mosques what to believe or how to worship or whom to employ as priests, rabbis, or imams. It punishes no one who rejects the Ten Commandments, no matter the reason. It levies no taxes to support any clergy. It does not co-opt churches to perform civic functions. These are the kinds of things “establishments of religion” did at the founding. SB 10 does none of them.

Graham was, arguing that it survived the abandonment of Lemon—and that if Stone is no longer good law, it’s for the Supreme Court to say so, not the Fifth Circuit.

Refugee and Immigrant Center for Education and Legal Services v. Mullin. In an opinion by Judge

United States v. California. In an opinion by Judge

Ream v. U.S. Department of the Treasury. In an opinion by Judge

United States v. Keirans. Here is John Ross’s summary for

United States v. Alsenat. In an opinion by Judge

Moore v. Senate Majority PAC. In an opinion by Judge

Staying on defamation but turning to the trial courts, it wasn’t a great week for plaintiffs:

Judge George C. Hanks Jr. (S.D. Tex.) dismissed FBI Director Kash Patel’s defamation lawsuit against former FBI official Frank Figliuzzi, who said on air, on Morning Joe, that Patel “has been visible at nightclubs far more than he has been on the seventh floor of the Hoover building.”

The 14-page indictment charges the SPLC with six counts of wire fraud, four counts of making false statements to a federally insured bank, and one count of conspiracy to commit concealment money laundering. The wire fraud counts focus on the alleged defrauding of donors, while the false statement counts are based on the SPLC opening bank accounts in the names of non-existent businesses (accounts that it used to make the payments, according to the indictment).
